A simplicial degree-2 ear of a 2-tree G is a vertex v with deg_G(v)=2 whose two neighbours are adjacent; then G-v is again a 2-tree. For such v set delta+(v) := s+(G) - s+(G-v) and delta-(v) := s-(G) - s-(G-v). Conjecture (L'): for every 2-tree G on n >= 4 vertices there exists a simplicial degree-2 ear v* with delta+(v*) >= 17/16 and delta-(v*) >= 17/16 (equivalently, by the trace identity delta+(v*) + delta-(v*) = 4, with delta-(v*) in [17/16, 47/16]).

rows.title: Max-degsum selector conjecture (refined selector, carried v8->v14)
rows.statement: With definitions as in row lprime, for a simplicial degree-2 ear v of a 2-tree G let {a,b} be its supporting edge (its two neighbours, which are adjacent) and define degsum(v) := deg_{G-v}(a) + deg_{G-v}(b). Conjecture: for every 2-tree G on n >= 4 vertices there exists a simplicial degree-2 ear v* attaining max{ degsum(v) : v a simplicial degree-2 ear of G } such that delta+(v*) >= 17/16 and delta-(v*) >= 17/16.

rows.title: Book-arm monotonicity for spider 2-trees (O5e.1)
rows.statement: For integers k1, k2, k3 >= 0, let S(k1,k2,k3) be the 2-tree on 3 + k1 + k2 + k3 vertices consisting of a triangle {x,y,z} together with k1 additional vertices adjacent exactly to {x,y}, k2 adjacent exactly to {y,z}, and k3 adjacent exactly to {x,z}. For k1 >= 1 define g(k1; k2,k3) := s-(S(k1,k2,k3)) - s-(S(k1-1,k2,k3)) (the delta- gain of deleting one arm-1 page ear), with s- as in row lprime. Claim: for all k2, k3 >= 0 and all k1 >= 2, g(k1; k2,k3) >= g(k1-1; k2,k3).

rows.title: Strong selector conjecture: degsum >= 6 forces delta- >= 17/16
rows.statement: With definitions as in rows lprime and maxdegsum-selector: for every 2-tree G on n >= 4 vertices and every simplicial degree-2 ear v of G with supporting edge {a,b} satisfying deg_{G-v}(a) + deg_{G-v}(b) >= 6, it holds that delta-(v) >= 17/16.

rows.title: First-difference limit delta-(L_n) -> (32*pi - 27*sqrt(3))/(12*pi)
rows.statement: With L_n and delta-(L_n) := s-(L_n) - s-(L_{n-1}) as in row two-path-floor-5c: lim_{n -> infinity} delta-(L_n) = (32*pi - 27*sqrt(3))/(12*pi) (approximately 1.42618).

rows.title: 2-path ear-gain floor: delta-(L_n) >= 17/16 for all n >= 4
rows.statement: Let L_n (the 2-path, the square of the path P_n) be the graph on vertex set {1,...,n} with i adjacent to j iff |i-j| in {1,2}; then L_n is a 2-tree and L_n minus the vertex n is L_{n-1}. Define delta-(L_n) := s-(L_n) - s-(L_{n-1}) with s- as in row lprime. Claim: for every integer n >= 4, delta-(L_n) >= 17/16.

rows.title: Universal ear-deletion lemma (L) — refuted by BT(k,2) tail ears
rows.statement: With definitions as in row lprime: for every 2-tree G on n >= 4 vertices and every simplicial degree-2 ear v of G, min(delta+(v), delta-(v)) >= 17/16. [REFUTED. Counterexample: the 2-tree BT(50,2) on n = 54 vertices — vertices 0,1 (book spine), 2..51 (book pages adjacent to both 0 and 1), plus edges {0,52},{2,52},{2,53},{52,53} — whose tail ear v = 53 (supporting edge {2,52}) has delta-(v) ~= 1.05751 < 17/16 = 1.0625; the BT(100,2) analogue on n = 104 has delta- ~= 1.04716.]

rows.title: Single-scalar W- threshold at 17/32 (retired Conjecture 7.1, natural instance) — refuted on 2-paths
rows.statement: With W-(v) := ||P- (e_a + e_b)||^2 defined as in row ansatz-a (P- the negative-eigenspace spectral projector of A(G-v)) and the max-degsum ear as in row maxdegsum-selector: for every 2-tree G on n >= 5 vertices, every simplicial degree-2 ear v* attaining the maximum degsum satisfies W-(v*) >= 17/32. [REFUTED. Counterexample: the 2-paths L_5 (W-(v*) ~= 0.5149 < 17/32 = 0.53125) and L_6 (W-(v*) ~= 0.3796); a fortiori the pre-renormalisation threshold W-(v*) >= 17/16 also fails.]

rows.title: Conjecture L (universal form)
rows.statement: For every 3-arc-strong directed multigraph D (every nonempty proper X ⊂ V has |δ+(X)| ≥ 3 with multiplicity), every root r, every pair of arc-disjoint spanning in-arborescences T, U rooted at r, and every arc a ∈ T, some arc b ∈ U ∩ δ+(X_a^T) satisfies X_b^U ∩ X_a^T ⊊ X_a^T, where X_a^T = {x : the unique T-path from x to r uses a}. DISPROVED by the 4-vertex funnel pair embedded in K4* (the complete bidirected digraph on {r, u, v1, w}; 12 arcs, λ_arc = 3): T = {(v1,u),(u,r),(w,r)}, U = {(u,v1),(v1,r),(w,v1)}, a = (u,r) ∈ T, X = X_a^T = {u,v1}; the unique U-exit from X is b = (v1,r) with X_b^U = {v1,u,w}, so X_b^U ∩ X = X, not a strict subset.

rows.title: Directed Karger cut-counting DK(α)
rows.statement: For every λ-arc-strong directed multigraph D on n ≥ 3 vertices and every α ≥ 1, the number of nonempty proper vertex sets X ⊊ V with |δ+(X)| ≤ α·λ (arc multiplicities counted) is at most n^(2α). DISPROVED at α = 2 by the asymmetric in/out-bundle: V = {s, o, p_1, …, p_k}; arcs (s,o) and (o,s) each with multiplicity 3, and for each toggle p_i: (s,p_i) with multiplicity 3 and (p_i,o) with multiplicity 3; oracle-certified λ_arc = 3. For every S ⊆ {p_1..p_k}, the set X = {o} ∪ S has δ+(X) = the three copies of (o,s) (each toggle in S contributes 0), i.e. |δ+(X)| = 3 = λ, giving 2^k distinct sets within the α = 2 band. At k = 17 (n = 19): the band count is 131378 > n^4 = 130321.

rows.title: Directed Karger arc-set counting DK*(α) for α ≥ 4/3
rows.statement: For every λ-arc-strong directed multigraph D on n vertices and every α ≥ 4/3, the number of DISTINCT out-cut arc-sets {δ+(X) : ∅ ≠ X ⊊ V, |δ+(X)| ≤ α·λ} is at most n^(2α). DISPROVED by the tagged-bundle family T(k, lam): V = {s, o, p_1, …, p_k} (n = k + 2); arcs (s,o)×lam, (o,s)×lam; per toggle p_i: (s,p_i)×lam, (p_i,o)×lam, plus ONE private tag arc (p_i,s)×1. The tag arcs make S ↦ δ+({o} ∪ S) injective as an arc-set map, so the number of distinct arc-sets of size ≤ α·λ is at least Σ_{j ≤ (α−1)·lam} C(k,j) = 2^Ω(n). Oracle-certified instances: T(18,10) (n = 20, λ = 10): 199329 distinct arc-sets of size ≤ 20 > n^4 = 160000 (α = 2), and 31216 > 21715 at α = 5/3; T(22,8) (n = 24, λ = 8): 600645 arc-sets of size ≤ 16 > n^4 = 331776. Both instances are SAD = SAT, and the arc-set first-moment mass Σ_F 2^(1−|F|) exceeds 1 (2.94 / 58.7), so the union-bound route genuinely fails there.

rows.title: α = 1 core: minimum out-cut arc-set count ≤ 2(n−1)²
rows.statement: For every λ-arc-strong directed multigraph D on n vertices, the number of distinct global minimum out-cut arc-sets {δ+(X) : ∅ ≠ X ⊊ V, |δ+(X)| = λ_arc(D)} is at most 2(n−1)² (a fortiori at most n²). DISPROVED by the family B_k: V = {s, t, p_1, …, p_k} (n = k + 2); arcs s→p_i (×1), p_i→t (×1), p_i→s (×(k−1)), t→p_i (×(k−1)), t→s (×k). Then λ_arc(B_k) = k and B_k has exactly 2^k + 2k distinct global minimum out-cut arc-sets, 2^k of them the binary family δ+({s} ∪ S) over S ⊆ {p_1..p_k}. At k = 8 (n = 10): 272 > 2(n−1)² = 162 > n² = 100. Symbolic proof for all k ≥ 2 (four-case cut computation) plus oracle-exact λ and exhaustive arc-set enumeration at k = 6, 8.

rows.title: EPK-2: two-coordinate Edmonds-packing determination of minimum cuts
rows.statement: For every λ-arc-strong directed multigraph D and every root r, there exist an Edmonds packing T_1, …, T_λ of pairwise arc-disjoint spanning in-arborescences rooted at r and indices i ≠ j such that the map F ↦ (F ∩ T_i, F ∩ T_j) is injective on the family of minimum out-cut arc-sets F = δ+(X) with r ∉ X. DISPROVED by pigeonhole on B_6 (family of row G24 at k = 6, n = 8, λ = 6): by the classical Transversal Lemma (every arc-disjoint spanning in-arborescence to r crosses each root-avoiding minimum out-cut exactly once, and F ⊆ ∪T_i), each signature (F ∩ T_i, F ∩ T_j) is a pair of single arcs, one from each of two fixed (n−1)-arc trees, hence takes at most (n−1)² = 49 values for ANY packing and index pair; but B_6 has 2^6 = 64 root-avoiding minimum out-cut arc-sets (70 in total, the binary family δ+({s} ∪ S) contributing 64) — so no Edmonds packing admits an injective pair.

rows.title: L-exist for general 3-arc-strong multidigraphs
rows.statement: For every 3-arc-strong directed multigraph D and every arc a ∈ A(D), there exists a pair of arc-disjoint spanning in-arborescences T, U rooted at head(a) with a ∈ T such that some b ∈ U ∩ δ+(X_a^T) satisfies X_b^U ∩ X_a^T ⊊ X_a^T — even restricted to instances with 2 ≤ |X_a^T| ≤ n − 2 (notation as in row G1). DISPROVED by the path-thickening of the path 1–0–2–3 (three parallel arcs in each direction on each of the three edges; 4 vertices, 18 arcs, Eulerian, λ_arc = 3, oracle-exact): with a = any copy of (0,2), rooted at head(a) = 2, every in-arborescence containing a has X_a^T = {0,1}; every arc leaving {0,1} has tail 0, so every U has exactly one exit from X and, by the one-exit criterion, no pair has a strict exit. All 216 arc-disjoint pairs (72 ordered pairs per labelled copy of a, pairs exist so not vacuous) fail, machine-verified.

rows.title: Chain Crossing Selection / Missing Entry Lemma (sealed multi-crossing chain kernels)
rows.statement: Setting: D• a 3-arc-strong chord-contraction multidigraph with fixed root ρ = the contracted vertex (class as in row H2-LEXIST). Let (T,U) be arc-disjoint spanning in-arborescences rooted at ρ and a = (u,v) ∈ T such that (T,U) is a hard gateway pair at a: X_a^T equals the canonical cage C = C_u (by the proved D12 cage canonicalization C1–C7, the vertex set of a t = u hard gateway is determined by u alone, independent of the pair) and U has a unique exit arc b = (u,y) from C, so no strict U-exit exists. Let A be the T-ancestor path from v to ρ, and for w ∉ C ∪ A let S_w = {x : the T-path from x to ρ passes through w}. A realizable chain kernel is such a configuration containing a sealed block B ⊇ C with δ+(B) ⊆ {(p_i, p_{i+1}) : p_i forced}, the forced crossing arcs of the unique shortest v→ρ path P_v (computed in D• − u), where p_i is forced iff (p_i, p_{i+1}) is p_i's unique out-arc in the outside digraph D_O (the subdigraph induced on the vertices outside C ∪ A ∪ {u}); a forced crossing is U-used iff its arc lies in U; the kernel is non-degenerate (sealed multi-crossing, per the D48 classifier) iff at least two forced crossings are U-used. CONJECTURE (Missing Entry Lemma): in every realizable non-degenerate in-class chain kernel with hard gateway pair at C there is a U-used forced crossing tail s, or a T-ancestor w of such a tail inside the sealed block, satisfying: (1) w ∉ A; (2) there is an arc d = (w,c) with c ∈ C and d ∉ U (a U-free entry into the cage); (3) [y ∉ C ∪ S_w] + #{s' ∈ S_w : the U-out-arc of s' has head outside C ∪ S_w} ≥ 2. (By the proved Lemma B3+, conditions (1)-(3) imply that replacing the T-out-arc of w by d yields a good pair — a strict U-exit at a — with U unchanged; so this lemma closes the chain-kernel case of L-exist branch 2.)

rows.title: L-exist (chord-contraction class, fixed contracted root)
rows.statement: Let D be a simple 3-arc-strong (1,0)-near-split digraph: V(D) = V1 ⊔ V2, D[V2] semicomplete (every two distinct vertices of V2 joined by an arc in at least one direction), |V2| ≥ 3, and V1 carrying exactly one internal arc e0 = (p,q) ('3-arc-strong' = every nonempty proper X ⊂ V has |δ+(X)| ≥ 3, arcs counted with multiplicity). Let D• be the chord-contraction multidigraph obtained from D by contracting e0 to a single vertex r (identify p,q; delete loops; keep parallel arcs), and assume λ_arc(D•) ≥ 3. For an in-arborescence T rooted at r (a spanning arc set giving every vertex ≠ r exactly one out-arc, with all T-paths leading to r) and an arc a ∈ T, write X_a^T = {x ∈ V(D•) : the unique T-path from x to r uses a}. CONJECTURE: for every arc a ∈ A(D•) there exist arc-disjoint spanning in-arborescences T, U of D• rooted at r with a ∈ T such that, whenever 2 ≤ |X_a^T| ≤ |V(D•)| − 2, some arc b ∈ U ∩ δ+(X_a^T) satisfies X_b^U ∩ X_a^T ⊊ X_a^T (equivalently, by the proved one-exit criterion Lemma 2.1, |U ∩ δ+(X_a^T)| ≥ 2).
rows.notes: Plan coverage target 'L-exist' — present. Companion 'L-swap' (ledger H3-LSWAP) was skipped by the miner; see coverage_gaps.

rows.title: Constant-λ Eulerian strong-arc-decomposition theorem
rows.statement: There exists an absolute constant c such that every Eulerian directed multigraph D (d+(v) = d−(v) for every vertex v) with λ_arc(D) ≥ c (every nonempty proper X ⊂ V has |δ+(X)| ≥ c, arc multiplicities counted) admits a strong arc decomposition (SAD): a partition A(D) = A1 ⊔ A2 such that both spanning subdigraphs (V, A1) and (V, A2) are strongly connected. Falsifiable instance form: a single Eulerian c-arc-strong (for the fixed c, e.g. c = 3) digraph with no SAD refutes the c-instance and, at c = 3, refutes WC3 in the Eulerian world.
rows.notes: References WC3 at c=3 (Eulerian world) but does not state it; WC3 itself is a coverage gap.

rows.title: WC3: every 3-arc-strong digraph has a strong arc decomposition
rows.statement: Every 3-arc-strong digraph D (every non-trivial out-cut has at least 3 arcs) admits a strong arc decomposition: a partition A(D) = A1 ⊔ A2 such that both spanning subdigraphs (V(D), A1) and (V(D), A2) are strongly connected. (The K = 3 working instance of the Bang-Jensen–Yeo conjecture; all known obstructions are exactly 2-arc-strong, so 3-arc-strength is conjectured sufficient.)
rows.notes: Minted at X0c consolidation (coverage-gap fix: plan-named target absent from ledger live_hypotheses). The pre-existing digraph-theory WC3 constant reconciles onto this row at X1.

rows.title: Conjecture P: every 2-extremal digraph has planar underlying graph
rows.statement: Setting: D a finite loopless digraph without parallel arcs; digon = pair of opposite arcs; U(D) = underlying simple undirected graph; lambda(D) = max over ordered pairs (x,y) of the max number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed x->y paths; chi_vec(D) = least t such that V(D) partitions into t classes each inducing an acyclic subdigraph; D is 2-extremal iff D is strongly connected, U(D) is 2-connected, lambda(D) = 2 and chi_vec(D) = 3. CLAIM: for every 2-extremal digraph D, U(D) is planar.
rows.notes: Verified distinct from parent (necessary-condition form, strictly weaker than the Section-9 characterization Conjecture 9.2).

rows.title: Interface-width merge lemma (refuted): width >= 3 per side of a digon-free 2/2 cut forces 2-dicolourability
rows.statement: Setting: D a finite loopless digraph without parallel arcs; digon = pair of opposite arcs; single arc = arc uv with vu absent; D Eulerian = in-degree equals out-degree at every vertex; lambda(D) = max over ordered pairs of the max number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed paths; chi_vec(D) = least t with V(D) partitioned into t classes inducing acyclic subdigraphs. For a digon-free cut (S, V(D)\S) (both sides nonempty, no digon crossing), the crossing arcs are the arcs with one end in each side, and the interface-width of a side is the number of distinct endpoints of crossing arcs lying on that side. REFUTED CLAIM: if D is Eulerian with lambda(D) = 2 and has a digon-free cut with exactly two crossing arcs S -> V(D)\S, exactly two crossing arcs V(D)\S -> S, and interface-width >= 3 on each side, then chi_vec(D) <= 2. (Companion refuted claim, killed by the same witness: for such digraphs, interface-width >= 3 on each side of such a cut implies U(D) is 3-connected.)

rows.title: P4 overlap target: the overlapping pure-point four-cycle cover is impossible at k(F_D)=3
rows.statement: Setting: D a finite loopless digraph without parallel arcs; digon = pair of opposite arcs uv,vu; single arc = arc uv with vu absent; U(D) = underlying simple graph; F_D = undirected graph on V(D) with edges the digon pairs; lambda(D) = max over ordered pairs of the max number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed paths; chi_vec(D) = least t with V(D) partitioned into t classes inducing acyclic subdigraphs; D is 2-extremal iff D is strongly connected, U(D) is 2-connected, lambda(D)=2 and chi_vec(D)=3. For 2-extremal D, F_D is a forest (proved in-folder, docs/lemma_a.md), so if F_D has exactly 3 connected components (isolated vertices counted) its proper 2-colourings (digon ends coloured differently) modulo global colour swap form the flip set Omega with |Omega| = 4. Let S be the spanning subdigraph of D consisting of all single arcs. A directed cycle of S is BAD AT x in Omega if all its vertices receive one colour under x, and FULL-SUPPORT if it has vertices in all three F_D-components. CLAIM: there is no 2-extremal digraph D with U(D) 3-connected and F_D having exactly 3 components such that (a) no bad dicycle of S has vertices in exactly two F_D-components, (b) there exist four full-support dicycles {C_x : x in Omega}, C_x bad at x, with union of arc sets equal to A(S), and (c) the C_x are not pairwise arc-disjoint, i.e. the overlap circulation Sum_{x in Omega} 1_{C_x} - 1_S is nonzero. Equivalently, such a configuration forces lambda(D) >= 3, contradicting lambda(D) = 2.

rows.title: Step 1 for k>=4: a 3-connected 2-extremal digraph has fewer than 4 digon-forest components
rows.statement: Setting: D a finite loopless digraph without parallel arcs; digon = pair of opposite arcs; U(D) = underlying simple undirected graph; F_D = undirected graph on V(D) whose edges are the digon pairs; k(F_D) = number of connected components of F_D on the full vertex set V(D), isolated vertices included; lambda(D) = max over ordered pairs (x,y) of the max number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed x->y paths; chi_vec(D) = least t such that V(D) partitions into t classes each inducing an acyclic subdigraph; D is 2-extremal iff D is strongly connected, U(D) is 2-connected, lambda(D) = 2 and chi_vec(D) = 3. CLAIM: there is no 2-extremal digraph D with U(D) 3-connected and k(F_D) >= 4.

rows.title: Connectivity-form hub barrier (refuted): uniform 4-edge-connectivity forces a connected digon forest
rows.statement: Setting: D a finite loopless digraph without parallel arcs; digon = pair of opposite arcs uv,vu; single arc = arc uv with vu absent; U(D) = underlying simple undirected graph; F_D = undirected graph on V(D) with edges the digon pairs; D Eulerian = in-degree equals out-degree at every vertex; lambda(D) = max over ordered pairs of the max number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed paths. Let M be the undirected multigraph obtained from U(D) by replacing each digon {u,v} by two parallel u-v edges and each single arc by one edge; M is uniformly 4-edge-connected if every pair of distinct vertices is joined by exactly 4 pairwise edge-disjoint paths in M. REFUTED CLAIM: if U(D) is 3-connected, D is Eulerian, lambda(D) = 2, and M is uniformly 4-edge-connected, then F_D is connected (equivalently, D has no digon-free cut).

rows.title: Criticality barrier: a 3-connected lambda=2 Eulerian digraph with a digon-free cut is 2-dicolourable
rows.statement: Let D be a finite loopless digraph with no parallel arcs (a digon is a pair of opposite arcs uv and vu). Let U(D) be its underlying simple undirected graph and F_D the undirected graph on V(D) whose edges are exactly the vertex pairs joined by a digon of D. For an ordered pair (x,y), lambda(x,y) is the maximum number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed x->y paths, and lambda(D) = max over all ordered pairs (x,y) of lambda(x,y). The dichromatic number chi_vec(D) is the least t such that V(D) can be partitioned into t classes each inducing an acyclic subdigraph. D is Eulerian if in-degree equals out-degree at every vertex. A digon-free cut is a bipartition (S, V(D)\S) with both sides nonempty such that no digon has one end in each side; such a cut exists iff F_D is disconnected as a graph on all of V(D) (isolated vertices count as components). CLAIM: for every such D, if (i) U(D) is 3-connected, (ii) D is Eulerian, (iii) lambda(D) = 2, and (iv) D has a digon-free cut, then chi_vec(D) <= 2. Equivalently: if U(D) is 3-connected, D is Eulerian, lambda(D) = 2 and chi_vec(D) = 3, then F_D is connected.

rows.title: No internal monochromatic single-arc dicycle in any digon-forest component
rows.statement: Setting as in H6: D a finite loopless digraph without parallel arcs; digon = pair of opposite arcs; a single arc is an arc uv with vu not in A(D); F_D = undirected graph on V(D) whose edges are the digon pairs; lambda(D) = max over ordered pairs (x,y) of the max number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed x->y paths; Eulerian = in-degree equals out-degree at every vertex; a digon-free cut is a bipartition of V(D) with both sides nonempty crossed by no digon (exists iff F_D is disconnected). A proper 2-colouring of a connected component T of F_D assigns colours {0,1} to V(T) so that the two ends of every F_D-edge in T differ (if T is a tree these are exactly its canonical bipartition and the swap). CLAIM: for every such D with (i) U(D) 3-connected, (ii) D Eulerian, (iii) lambda(D) = 2, and (iv) F_D disconnected, the following holds for every connected component T of F_D and every proper 2-colouring c of T: D contains no directed cycle C such that every arc of C is a single arc, every vertex of C lies in V(T), and all vertices of C receive the same colour under c. (chi_vec(D) = 3 is NOT assumed.)

rows.title: Step 1 (ledger open_crux): 3-connected 2-extremal implies F_D is a spanning tree
rows.statement: Setting: D a finite loopless digraph without parallel arcs; digon = pair of opposite arcs; U(D) = underlying simple undirected graph; F_D = undirected graph on V(D) whose edges are the digon pairs; lambda(D) = max over ordered pairs of the max number of pairwise arc-disjoint directed paths; chi_vec(D) = least t with V(D) partitioned into t classes inducing acyclic subdigraphs; D is 2-extremal iff D is strongly connected, U(D) is 2-connected, lambda(D) = 2 and chi_vec(D) = 3. A digon-free cut of D is a bipartition (S, V(D)\S), both sides nonempty, crossed by no digon. CLAIM: for every 2-extremal digraph D with U(D) 3-connected, F_D is a spanning tree of U(D), i.e. F_D is connected on all of V(D) (equivalently, since F_D is a forest for 2-extremal D — proved in-folder — U(D) has no digon-free cut).

rows.title: BST-orderings are free on the S_k tower (bstOmega(S_k) = omegaVec(S_k) for k <= 4)
rows.statement: For every k in {1,2,3,4}, bstOmega(S_k) = omegaVec(S_k). Definitions: S_1 = TT1 (one-vertex tournament), S_k = Delta(TT1, S_{k-1}, S_{k-1}), where Delta(A,B,C) is the substitution of tournaments A, B, C into the directed triangle (disjoint union of V(A), V(B), V(C), internal arcs kept, plus all arcs a->b, b->c, c->a for a in V(A), b in V(B), c in V(C)). For a linear ordering prec of V(T), the back-edge graph of (T,prec) is the undirected graph on V(T) with an edge {u,w} for u prec w iff T contains the arc w->u; omegaVec(T) is the minimum over ALL linear orderings prec of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec). A BST-ordering of T is defined recursively: choose a root r in V(T); the ordering is (a BST-ordering of the subtournament induced on N^-(r) = {u : u->r}), then r, then (a BST-ordering of the subtournament induced on N^+(r) = {u : r->u}); bstOmega(T) is the minimum over BST-orderings prec of T of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec) (so bstOmega(T) >= omegaVec(T) always).

rows.title: chiVec = omegaVec on prime twin-width-<=1 tournaments
rows.statement: Every prime tournament T with tww(T) <= 1 satisfies chiVec(T) = omegaVec(T). Definitions: a tournament is a finite loopless directed graph with exactly one arc between every pair of distinct vertices. A module of T is a vertex set M such that every vertex v outside M satisfies either v->m in T for all m in M, or m->v in T for all m in M; T is prime if it has no module M with 1 < |M| < |V(T)|. chiVec(T), the dichromatic number, is the least r such that V(T) can be partitioned into r sets each inducing an acyclic subtournament. omegaVec(T) is the minimum over all linear orderings prec of V(T) of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec), the undirected graph on V(T) with an edge {u,w} for u prec w iff T contains the arc w->u. tww(T) (twin-width) is defined via contraction sequences: a sequence of partitions of V(T) starting from singletons, each step merging two parts, ending in one part; at any step two distinct parts P, P' carry a red edge unless they are homogeneous (every arc of T between P and P' goes from P to P', or every such arc goes from P' to P); the width of a sequence is the maximum over steps and parts of the number of red edges at a part, and tww(T) is the minimum width over all contraction sequences.

rows.title: Transitive-middle substitution tower R_k keeps omegaVec <= 2
rows.statement: For every k >= 1, omegaVec(R_k) <= 2, where the tournaments R_k are defined by R_1 = TT1 (the one-vertex tournament) and R_k = Delta(R_{k-1}, TT_{k-1}, R_{k-1}); here TT_m is the transitive tournament on m vertices (vertices 1..m with arc i->j iff i < j), and Delta(A,B,C) is the substitution of tournaments A, B, C into the directed triangle: the tournament on the disjoint union of V(A), V(B), V(C) keeping all arcs internal to A, B, C, plus every arc a->b, b->c, c->a for a in V(A), b in V(B), c in V(C). omegaVec(T) is the minimum over all linear orderings prec of V(T) of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec), the undirected graph on V(T) with an edge {u,w} for u prec w iff T contains the arc w->u. (Proposed so that {R_k} — with twin-width 1 and growing dichromatic number chiVec(R_k) — would be a bounded-omegaVec counterexample family to Conjecture 3.12.)

rows.title: T* = Delta(C3, S_3, TT1) as a bounded-omegaVec, chiVec>=4 witness
rows.statement: The 11-vertex tournament T* = Delta(C3, S_3, TT1) satisfies omegaVec(T*) <= 2 and chiVec(T*) >= 4. Definitions: Delta(A,B,C) is the substitution of tournaments A, B, C into the directed triangle: the tournament on the disjoint union of V(A), V(B), V(C) keeping all arcs internal to A, B, C, plus every arc a->b, b->c, c->a for a in V(A), b in V(B), c in V(C); C3 is the directed 3-cycle; TT1 is the one-vertex tournament; S_3 = Delta(TT1, C3, C3) (7 vertices, from the tower S_1 = TT1, S_k = Delta(TT1, S_{k-1}, S_{k-1})). chiVec(T), the dichromatic number, is the least r such that V(T) can be partitioned into r sets each inducing an acyclic subtournament; omegaVec(T) is the minimum over all linear orderings prec of V(T) of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec), the undirected graph on V(T) with an edge {u,w} for u prec w iff T contains the arc w->u.

rows.title: Emptiness of the (tww<=1, omegaVec<=2, chiVec>=4) cell at the Neumann-Lara floor n=11
rows.statement: Every tournament T on at most 11 vertices with tww(T) <= 1 and omegaVec(T) <= 2 satisfies chiVec(T) <= 3. Definitions: a tournament is a finite loopless directed graph with exactly one arc between every pair of distinct vertices. chiVec(T), the dichromatic number, is the least r such that V(T) can be partitioned into r sets each inducing an acyclic subtournament. For a linear ordering prec of V(T), the back-edge graph of (T,prec) is the undirected graph on V(T) with an edge {u,w} for u prec w iff T contains the arc w->u; omegaVec(T) is the minimum over all linear orderings prec of V(T) of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec). tww(T) (twin-width) is defined via contraction sequences: a sequence of partitions of V(T) starting from singletons, each step merging two parts, ending in one part; at any step two distinct parts P, P' carry a red edge unless they are homogeneous (every arc of T between P and P' goes from P to P', or every such arc goes from P' to P); the width of a sequence is the maximum over steps and parts of the number of red edges at a part, and tww(T) is the minimum width over all contraction sequences.

rows.title: omegaVec-only recursion for the C3-substitution lift Delta(1,B,B)
rows.statement: There exists a function h from the positive integers to the positive integers such that omegaVec(Delta(1,B,B)) = h(omegaVec(B)) for every tournament B; equivalently, for all tournaments B, B', if omegaVec(B) = omegaVec(B') then omegaVec(Delta(1,B,B)) = omegaVec(Delta(1,B',B')). Definitions: a tournament is a finite loopless directed graph with exactly one arc between every pair of distinct vertices. For a linear ordering prec of V(T), the back-edge graph of (T,prec) is the undirected graph on V(T) with an edge {u,w} for u prec w iff T contains the arc w->u; omegaVec(T) is the minimum over all linear orderings prec of V(T) of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec). Delta(A,B,C) is the substitution of tournaments A, B, C into the directed triangle: the tournament on the disjoint union of V(A), V(B), V(C) keeping all arcs internal to A, B and C, plus every arc a->b, b->c and c->a for a in V(A), b in V(B), c in V(C). Delta(1,B,B) abbreviates Delta(TT1,B,B), TT1 being the one-vertex tournament.

rows.title: Bounded BST-penalty (restricted Conjecture 3.16) on closure-outside twin-width-1 tournaments
rows.statement: There exists a function g from the positive integers to the positive integers such that bstOmega(T) <= g(omegaVec(T)) for every tournament T with tww(T) <= 1 that does not belong to {TT1,TT2,C3}^subst. Definitions: a tournament is a finite loopless directed graph with exactly one arc between every pair of distinct vertices. For a linear ordering prec of V(T), the back-edge graph of (T,prec) is the undirected graph on V(T) with an edge {u,w} for u prec w iff T contains the arc w->u; omegaVec(T) is the minimum over all linear orderings prec of V(T) of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec). A BST-ordering of T is defined recursively: choose a root r in V(T); the ordering is (a BST-ordering of the subtournament induced on N^-(r) = {u : u->r in T}), then r, then (a BST-ordering of the subtournament induced on N^+(r) = {u : r->u in T}); bstOmega(T) is the minimum over BST-orderings prec of T of the clique number of the back-edge graph of (T,prec) (BST-orderings form a subset of all orderings, so bstOmega(T) >= omegaVec(T)). tww(T) (twin-width) is defined via contraction sequences: a sequence of partitions of V(T) starting from singletons, each step merging two parts, ending in one part; at any step two distinct parts P, P' carry a red edge unless they are homogeneous (every arc of T between P and P' goes from P to P', or every such arc goes from P' to P); the width of a sequence is the maximum over steps and parts of the number of red edges at a part, and tww(T) is the minimum width over all contraction sequences. {TT1,TT2,C3}^subst is the smallest class of tournaments containing TT1 (one vertex), TT2 (two vertices, one arc) and C3 (the directed 3-cycle) that is closed under substitution: replacing a vertex v of a member T by a member U (each vertex of U inheriting v's arcs to V(T)\{v}) yields a member.
rows.notes: Attack-restricted form of Conjecture 3.16 — verified NOT a duplicate of the full-3.16 S2 row axv_2310_04265_04. Folder parent = Conjecture 3.12 (axv_2310_04265_02).

rows.title: C = 2 deletion bound for internal vertices (unavoidability)
rows.statement: For every finite acyclic digraph D and every vertex v of D having at least one in-neighbour and at least one out-neighbour (an 'internal' vertex), unvd(D) <= 2 * unvd(D - v). Here a tournament is an orientation of a complete graph (exactly one arc between every pair of distinct vertices); a tournament T contains a digraph H if there is an injective map f: V(H) -> V(T) such that (u,w) in A(H) implies (f(u),f(w)) in A(T); and for a nonempty acyclic digraph H, unvd(H) (the unavoidability of H) is the least integer p >= 1 such that every tournament on p vertices contains H (finite for every acyclic H). D - v denotes the digraph obtained from D by deleting v and all arcs incident to v.
rows.notes: Verified distinct from parent Conjecture 9 (constant pinned to 2, restricted to internal vertices; with the paper's Prop 1 it would close Conjecture 9 with optimal constant).

rows.title: Flower snarks J_{2k+1} (k >= 3) are cyclically >= 6-edge-connected
rows.statement: For every integer k >= 3, the flower snark J_{2k+1} (defined as in row flower-family) has cyclic edge-connectivity at least 6, where the cyclic edge-connectivity of a cubic graph containing two vertex-disjoint cycles is the minimum size of an edge cut whose removal leaves at least two components each containing a cycle. (Companion finite fact, machine-verified in the folder: J_5 has cyclic edge-connectivity exactly 5.)

rows.title: Flower snarks J_{2k+1} all admit S2-flows (infinite-family target)
rows.statement: For every integer k >= 2, the flower snark J_{2k+1} admits an S2-flow. Definitions: for odd n = 2k+1, the flower snark J_n is the cubic graph on vertex set {a_i, b_i, c_i, d_i : i in Z/n} with edges a_i b_i, a_i c_i, a_i d_i (spokes, for every i), b_i b_{i+1 mod n} (the b-cycle), c_i c_{i+1} and d_i d_{i+1} for 0 <= i <= n-2, and the two cross edges c_{n-1} d_0 and d_{n-1} c_0 (so c_0 ... c_{n-1} d_0 ... d_{n-1} form a single 2n-cycle); J_n is simple, cubic, bridgeless, of girth 5 for n >= 5, and not 3-edge-colourable. Fix a reference orientation of each edge (for e = {u,v} with u < v set sigma(u,e) = +1, sigma(v,e) = -1). An S2-flow on a graph G is a map phi : E(G) -> S2 = {x in R^3 : |x| = 1} such that for every vertex v, sum over edges e incident to v of sigma(v,e) * phi(e) = 0 in R^3 (Kirchhoff conservation).

rows.title: Uniform shuttle CDC for flower snarks (refuted parametric template)
rows.statement: [REFUTED HYPOTHESIS] For every odd n = 2k+1 >= 5, the flower snark J_n (defined as in row flower-family) admits a cycle double cover (a multiset of cycles covering every edge exactly twice) in which every cycle passing through a star vertex a_i is a length-6 shuttle, where the shuttle of type bc at index i is the 6-cycle a_i b_i b_{i+1} a_{i+1} c_{i+1} c_i and the types bd and cd are defined analogously (indices mod n; at the wrap-around index the shuttle uses the cross edges c_{n-1} d_0 / d_{n-1} c_0). Concrete candidates refuted: v1 = {b-cycle, cd-cycle, all bc-shuttles, all bd-shuttles} and v2 = {all bc-shuttles, all bd-shuttles with cross-edge wrap-around} (scripts/flower_cdc.py).

rows.title: Z/(2n)-equivariant S2-flow on flower snarks (refuted ansatz)
rows.statement: [REFUTED HYPOTHESIS] For odd n = 2k+1 >= 5, the flower snark J_n (defined as in row flower-family) admits a Z/(2n)-equivariant S2-flow: there exist a rotation R in SO(3) of order exactly 2n (rotation by pi/n about some axis) and unit vectors u_b, u_c, w_b, w_c in S2 such that the assignment phi(a_i b_i) = R^i u_b, phi(a_i c_i) = R^i u_c, phi(a_i d_i) = R^{n+i} u_c, phi(b_i b_{i+1 mod n}) = R^i w_b (0 <= i <= n-1), and phi(sigma^j(c_0 c_1)) = R^j w_c (0 <= j <= 2n-1, where sigma is the order-2n graph automorphism of J_n shifting the cd-cycle by one step and the a/b indices by one mod n) is an S2-flow on J_n.

rows.title: No Z/n-equivariant S2-flow on flower snarks (nonexistence, uncertified)
rows.statement: For every odd n in {5, 7, 9, 11, 13} (working hypothesis: every odd n >= 5), the flower snark J_n (defined as in row flower-family) admits no partially Z/n-equivariant S2-flow. Precisely: there exist NO unit vectors u_b, u_c, u_d, w_b, w_c, w_d, x_cd, x_dc in S2 and unit axis a in S2, with R in SO(3) the rotation by angle 2*pi/n about a, satisfying the system: u_b + u_c + u_d = 0; u_b = (I - R^{-1}) w_b; u_c = (I - R^{-1}) w_c; u_d = (I - R^{-1}) w_d; u_c + x_dc = w_c; u_d + x_cd = w_d; R^{n-1} u_c + R^{n-2} w_c = x_cd; R^{n-1} u_d + R^{n-2} w_d = x_dc. A solution of this system is exactly an S2-flow on J_n of the form phi(a_i b_i) = R^i u_b, phi(a_i c_i) = R^i u_c, phi(a_i d_i) = R^i u_d, phi(b_i b_{i+1 mod n}) = R^i w_b, phi(c_i c_{i+1}) = R^i w_c and phi(d_i d_{i+1}) = R^i w_d (0 <= i <= n-2), with standalone values x_cd on cross edge c_{n-1} d_0 and x_dc on cross edge d_{n-1} c_0 (33 scalar equations in 27 real variables, over-determined by 6).

rows.title: Some snark admits an oriented 4-CDC (refuted route hypothesis)
rows.statement: [REFUTED HYPOTHESIS] There exists a snark (a simple connected cubic bridgeless graph with chromatic index 4) admitting an oriented 4-cycle double cover, i.e. a collection of exactly 4 directed cycles such that every edge is covered exactly twice and the two covering cycles traverse it in opposite directions. (This was the enabling hypothesis of the first-draft working theorem 'if a snark admits an oriented 4-CDC then it admits an S2-flow' - formally true but vacuous, since the hypothesis is unsatisfiable.)

rows.title: Blanusa-1 admits a weighted-CDC Sigma_4 flow (abstraction-gap probe)
rows.statement: The first Blanusa snark B1 (one of the two nontrivial snarks on 18 vertices; a nontrivial snark is a simple connected cubic bridgeless graph with chromatic index 4, girth >= 5, cyclically 4-edge-connected) admits a weighted-CDC Sigma_4-flow: there exist a cycle double cover C = {C_1, ..., C_t} of B1 (a multiset of cycles such that every edge lies in exactly two members), a direction for each cycle C_k, and vectors w_1, ..., w_t in R^4 each satisfying sum_{i=1}^4 (w_k)_i = 0, such that for every edge e - covered by exactly the two cycles C_i and C_j - the vector v_e := sigma_{e,i} w_i + sigma_{e,j} w_j lies in Sigma_4 := {x in R^4 : sum_i x_i = 0, sum_i x_i^2 = 2}, where sigma_{e,k} = +1 if the directed cycle C_k traverses e in the direction of the fixed reference orientation of e and -1 otherwise. (Sigma_4 is the radius-sqrt(2) sphere in the hyperplane sum x_i = 0, isomorphic to R^3, so any solution projects to an S2-flow on B1.)
